attribution.name: Michael Dummett
attribution.note: A/B/C trump-order classification ('The Game of Tarot') — the scholarly backbone

attribution.name: Tarot History Forum
attribution.note: Scholarly community consulted for genealogy (facts only; no text reproduced)

name: The Tree of Tarot — A Grammar of Tarot Grammars
description:
The genealogy of tarot as a single explorable tree: the decks ARE the cards. Built to render in the recursive.eco tree-viewer, with L1 = individual decks/traditions, L2 = the branches (Michael Dummett's A / B / C trump-orders, plus the occult reframing and the sui-generis relatives), and L3 = the root 'The Tree of Tarot'.

It corrects a common misconception: the Tarot de Marseille is NOT descended from the Bolognese Tarocchino — they are cousins, both descending from the mid-1400s Italian trionfi. Each leaf links to its full deck in this library (built, live in Supabase, or planned).

This is the capstone of the tarot collection: a map you can walk from the 1440s Milanese game decks, through the three regional orders and the printed Marseille standard, into the occult reframing that invented divination and produced the modern decks.

sections.What it is: The oldest near-complete tarot — 74 of 78 cards survive, painted by the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan. A game deck; four cards (Devil, Tower, Knight of Coins, Three of Swords) are lost.
sections.Lineage: Seeds the C-order. Sister to the Cary-Yale and Brera-Brambilla decks.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Pierpont-Morgan-Bergamo Visconti-Sforza Tarot (Milan, c. 1451) shows the hallmarks of this luxury painted deck: an elaborate figurative composition — a trump card or court figure — executed in tempera with gold and silver leaf on a richly decorated card. The Bembo workshop's work for the Sforza court produces images of aristocratic splendor: crowned figures in brocaded robes, celestial personifications against starry grounds, allegorical compositions with heraldic detail. The line is refined and the color palette warm — red, blue, green, and the gleam of applied metalwork — making these cards as much devotional objects as gaming pieces. Of the original 78, 74 survive across multiple collections, making this the most complete early Italian tarot.
sections.Symbol: The Bembo workshop's goldleaf luxury — the archetype of the painted Italian tarot, 74 surviving cards at the root of the Western tradition.

sections.What it is: The most lavish hand-painted tarot, with SIX courts per suit (incl. female Horsewoman & Damsel) and the unique theological virtues Faith, Hope, Charity.
sections.Lineage: A root deck; its idiosyncrasies (extra virtues, six courts) show how unsettled the early form was.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot (Milan, c. 1441-1447, now at Yale's Beinecke Library) shows the earliest and most distinctive of the three Visconti decks. A trump or court card by Bonifacio Bembo — perhaps one of the three Theological Virtue cards unique to this deck (Faith, Hope, Charity), or one of the eight surviving trumps, or one of the unusual five-rank courts (including the Horsewoman and Damsel, female court ranks absent from later traditions). The card is painted in tempera on parchment with gold-leaf backgrounds, the figures in the elaborate court dress of 15th-century Milan, rendered against grounds of solid burnished gold.
sections.Symbol: The earliest Visconti deck — gold grounds, five-rank courts, and three Theological Virtues found nowhere else in tarot history.

sections.What it is: The third Bembo sister deck, surviving only as a fragment (~48 cards, just two trumps).
sections.Lineage: A root deck; fills out the Milanese hand-painted group.
sections.Scene:
The Brera-Brambilla Tarot is a fragmentary 15th-century Milanese deck, now divided between the Brera Picture Gallery in Milan and private collections. Like the Visconti-Sforza, it was hand-painted for the ruling court and represents the luxury end of early tarot production.

Only a small number of cards survive. The imagery is consistent with the Visconti-Sforza type but shows its own distinct hand. The Brera-Brambilla cards are significant to scholars because they preserve alternative solutions to iconographic problems that the more famous Visconti-Sforza deck solved differently, suggesting that early Milanese tarot iconography was still fluid and not yet standardised.

sections.What it is: The 62-card Bolognese game deck (pips 2–5 removed), with the distinctive four 'Papi' and the A-order trump sequence. The living southern game tradition.
sections.Lineage: A-order. A COUSIN of the Marseille, not its ancestor — both descend from the common Italian root.
sections.In this library: ✅ Built in this library (grammars/tarocchino-bologna) — structure-accurate; per-card images TODO.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Tarocchino Bolognese shows the distinctive 62-card game deck of Bologna: a double-ended court card or a trump in the unusual Bolognese arrangement. The Tarocchino removed the pip cards 2-5 from each suit, leaving only Ace, 6-10, and courts, while the trump sequence features the four Mori (Moors/Popes) at the top and collapses the standard allegorical sequence into a compressed hierarchy. The engraved and hand-colored style of surviving Tarocchino decks shows Bolognese artistic conventions — bold outlines, primary colors, and the double-ended format that allows cards to be read from either end, a practical adaptation for competitive gameplay.
sections.Symbol: The 62-card Bolognese game deck — double-ended courts, four Papi Mori, and the 2-5 pips removed, the most distinctively regional tarot variant.

sections.What it is: The 97-card Florentine expansion — adds the four elements, twelve zodiac signs, and the full set of seven virtues, plus five supreme 'Arie'.
sections.Lineage: A-order (Florentine). The most cosmological tarot.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Minchiate Fiorentina (Florence, 1860-1890 edition shown here) displays the most expansive tarot tradition ever produced: 41 trump cards illustrated with the Florentine engraving aesthetic of the period. Perhaps a zodiac card (one of the 12 zodiacal trumps that are unique to the Minchiate), or an elemental trump (Fire, Water, Earth, Air), or one of the theological or cardinal virtues that make the Minchiate the only tarot to include all seven virtues. The 19th-century Florentine edition uses woodblock or lithographic printing with hand-coloring, the figures rendered in a confident Italian commercial style with bold outlines and warm colors.
sections.Symbol: The 97-card Florentine game deck — 41 trumps including four elements, 12 zodiac signs, and seven virtues, the most cosmologically comprehensive tarot tradition.

sections.What it is: Sicily's small, unusual deck, with cards found nowhere else (the 'Fugitivo' and 'Miseria').
sections.Lineage: A-order offshoot, the southernmost branch.
sections.Scene:
The Tarocco Siciliano is the regional tarot tradition of Sicily — isolated from the mainland by geography and preserved in its own form until the early 20th century. It is one of the rarest and most distinctive regional variants in the history of the game.

The Sicilian deck used a 63-card structure (neither the standard 78 nor the Bolognese 62) and preserved trump orderings that appear to represent a very early — possibly pre-Marseille — tradition. The Knight figure was counted as a female card, and several iconographic choices survive nowhere else in the European tradition. The Tarocco Siciliano is now largely extinct as a living game but is of immense historical value as evidence of early tarot diffusion routes through the Mediterranean.

sections.What it is: A hand-painted deck of which 17 trumps survive at the BnF. Long mis-called the oldest tarot (and wrongly tied to a 1392 payment to Gringonneur).
sections.Lineage: B-order (Ferrarese). A documentary cornerstone of the eastern tradition.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Charles VI Tarot (c. 1470s-1480s, France or Italy, now at the Bibliothèque nationale de France) shows one of the 17 surviving painted cards from this enigmatic deck. Long misattributed to the court of Charles VI (r. 1380-1422) and to Jacquemin Gringonneur, the cards are now dated to the late 15th century. The surviving cards — including some of the most beautiful early tarot images — are painted on thick card with elaborate gilding and tempera in the French-Italian courtly style of the period. The images show a sophisticated allegorical programme, the figures in elaborate costume against richly decorated grounds.
sections.Symbol: The 17 surviving cards of the Charles VI — misattributed to Gringonneur but actually 1470s French-Italian luxury work, among the most beautiful early tarot images.

sections.What it is: Fragmentary hand-painted cards from the Este court of Ferrara.
sections.Lineage: B-order. Completes the eastern luxury-deck picture beside Visconti's western ones.
sections.Scene:
The d'Este Tarot (Estensi Tarocchi) is a fragmentary 15th-century deck made for the Este court at Ferrara around 1450 — contemporaneous with the earliest Visconti-Sforza cards but from the rival court that shaped the other great stream of early Italian tarot.

Only 16 cards survive, all court cards (kings, queens, and knights for the four suits). They are held at Yale's Beinecke Library as part of the Cary Collection. The Este tradition is important because Ferrara's tarot ordering (the "B-order") differs from the Milanese/Marseille order in the positioning of Justice and Strength — a difference that resurfaces when the Golden Dawn and Waite revive the B-order numbering in the early 20th century. The Este court also produced Boiardo's tarocchi poem, which describes a completely different allegorical tarot system for the same family.

sections.What it is: The canonical printed standard (Type II), a woodcut game deck whose 22 trumps fixed the names and images the whole occult era inherited.
sections.Lineage: C-order, the trunk. Descends from the Milanese standard; everything occult branches from it.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Nicolas Conver 1760 Tarot de Marseille — probably the World (Le Monde) or the Fool (Le Mat) — shows the canonical Marseille woodblock printing in its most authoritative form: bold, clean lines, the primary color palette of red, blue, yellow, and flesh-tone, the figures in the characteristic pose of the Marseille iconographic programme. The woodblock medium produces a confident, graphic quality distinct from the painterly refinement of the Italian luxury decks; the Conver is a commercial print of the highest quality, the product of a thriving Marseille playing card industry that supplied the French market.
sections.Symbol: The 1760 Nicolas Conver woodblock — the canonical Marseille, the definitive reference point for the classical tarot iconographic programme.

sections.What it is: The earlier Marseille pattern (Jean Noblet c. 1650, Jean Dodal 1701), slightly different from the later Conver 'Type II'.
sections.Lineage: C-order. The first crystallisation of the Marseille image.
sections.In this library: 🔨 Planned (fuller PD pip scans live here).
sections.Scene:
The Marseille Type I decks represent the earliest phase of the Marseille tradition — specifically the decks made in Paris in the mid-17th century before the style fully consolidated into what we recognise as the "standard" Marseille.

The key Type I decks are the Noblet (Paris, c. 1650) and the Dodal (Lyon, c. 1701). They share a family resemblance with the later Conver (1760) but preserve iconographic details — particularly in the Fool, the Bateleur, and the Lovers — that suggest a slightly different source than the Conver's immediate ancestors.

Type I Marseille is important because it represents the moment when tarot imagery, having evolved through Italian courts and German-speaking cities, was fixed into the woodcut-and-stencil format that would remain standard for two centuries. The Noblet and Dodal are the oldest Marseille-type decks that survive in complete or near-complete form.

sections.What it is: A Marseille variant that replaces the Popess and Pope with Juno and Jupiter — a Protestant-friendly redesign.
sections.Lineage: C-order sibling of the Marseille.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Besançon Tarot shows one of the most distinctive variants of the Marseille tradition: the Besançon type replaces the Papess (II) with Juno and the Pope (V) with Jupiter, adapting the trump sequence for markets (particularly in German-speaking regions and Alsace) where representations of the Pope and Popess were considered inappropriate or provocative. The Besançon deck otherwise follows the Marseille iconographic programme closely, its woodblock printing in the bold Marseille color palette adapted for the northeastern French and Swiss German market.
sections.Symbol: The Besançon variant — Juno and Jupiter replacing Papess and Pope, the Marseille tradition adapted for Protestant and German-speaking markets.

sections.What it is: The Flemish 'Tarot de Vandenborre', replacing the Popess and Pope with Bacchus and the Spanish Captain (Capitano Eracasse).
sections.Lineage: C-order sibling of the Marseille.
sections.Scene:
The Belgian Tarot — most famously represented by the Vandenborre deck printed in Brussels — is a Marseille-tradition deck with two distinctive substitutions that mark it as the product of Catholic Flanders rather than France.

In the Belgian tradition, the Papesse (High Priestess) and the Pope (Hierophant) — figures that represented a somewhat irreverent take on papal authority — were replaced by Juno and Jupiter, classical deities who served the same structural roles in the deck without the theological awkwardness. The same substitution appears in the Tarot de Besançon; the two regional variants may share a common origin in censorship pressure or simply in regional taste.

Otherwise the Belgian deck is a faithful Marseille-type deck, and its point-scoring game rules followed the same patterns as other regional French and Swiss tarot traditions.

sections.What it is: The Piedmontese playing pattern, a later Italian descendant of the Marseille type.
sections.Lineage: C-order, the living Italian game pattern.
sections.Scene:
The Tarocco Piemontese (Piedmontese Tarot) is the regional variant of the Marseille tradition used in the Piedmont region of northwestern Italy — the area most closely linked to France through geography, language, and political history.

The Piedmontese deck uses the standard 78 Marseille cards but with slight modifications in iconography that reflect both Italian and French printmaking conventions. It remained in use as a living game tradition in Piedmont long after the Marseille deck had become primarily an occult object in France. The Piedmontese tarot game (Scarto) is one of the few traditional tarot games still played in Italy today.

The Piedmontese tradition bridges the Marseille and Italian branches: it uses the French-origin imagery but is played in an Italian game context with Italian suit-counting conventions.

sections.What it is: A Milanese neoclassical-revival tarot first produced in 1810 by the Gumppenberg firm — the elegant Italian engraved reworking of the Marseille pattern.
sections.Lineage: C-order; the Lombard/Italian engraved line that the Soprafino refines.
sections.Scene:
The Ancient Tarot of Lombardy, printed by the Gumppenberg firm in Milan in the 19th century, is a Marseille-tradition deck produced at a time when Italian printmakers were reproducing older French and Swiss models rather than developing new imagery.

The Gumppenberg deck is significant because it represents the continuity of the Marseille type into the industrial printing era — the same woodcut-derived imagery, now reproduced by engraving and lithography for a mass market. It shows that the Marseille tradition was understood by 19th-century Milanese publishers as a heritage to be preserved rather than a living design tradition to be evolved.

The deck also marks the moment when northern Italian tarot was beginning to be reabsorbed into the French occultist framing — at the same time these cards were being printed in Milan, Etteilla's disciples in Paris were redesigning the tarot entirely.

sections.What it is: Carlo Della Rocca’s finely engraved Soprafino deck of 1835 — the high point of the Italian engraved tarot and the model behind many later “Ancient Italian” reproductions.
sections.Lineage: C-order; the refined Lombard engraved pattern descending from the Gumppenberg line.
sections.Scene:
The Soprafino Tarot, designed by Carlo Della Rocca and printed in Milan in the 1830s and 1840s, is one of the most beautiful Marseille-tradition decks ever produced — "soprafino" means "superfine" or "ultra-refined," a designation of print quality that became attached to this deck.

The Della Rocca Soprafino is significant because it represents the apex of the Marseille tradition as a fine-printing object. At the same moment that French occultists were radically redesigning the tarot for divinatory purposes, Milanese craftsmen were perfecting the rendering of the traditional imagery. The Soprafino's engravings achieve a level of detail and tonal range impossible in woodcut printing, while preserving every conventional element of the Marseille iconography.

This deck is one of the favourite sources for modern "neo-Marseille" artists and designers who seek to work within the tradition rather than break from it.

sections.What it is: Antoine Court de Gébelin's engravings in 'Le Monde primitif', which first claimed the tarot was the ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth'. The myth that launched occult tarot.
sections.Lineage: The hinge: re-reads the C-order Marseille as esoteric wisdom.
sections.Scene: The representative image associated with Antoine Court de Gébelin's tarot work comes from his 1781 volume of the 'Monde Primitif' — the engraving of the World card that he published as evidence of the tarot's supposed Egyptian origins. Court de Gébelin's illustration is not a playing card but a scholarly plate in a Masonic-inflected encyclopedic work: an engraved version of a Marseille-type World card, printed in the black-and-white of 18th-century book illustration. This image marks the birth of occult tarot: the moment a game deck was reinterpreted as an ancient Egyptian Book of Thoth, launching the esoteric tradition that would define tarot for the next 250 years.
sections.Symbol: Court de Gébelin's 1781 World card engraving — the moment the game deck became the Book of Thoth, the foundational misattribution that launched occult tarot.

sections.What it is: The FIRST deck designed for divination — Jean-Baptiste Alliette's renamed, renumbered cards with a cosmogony sequence, significators, and systematic reversed meanings.
sections.Lineage: First fruit of the occult branch; descends from the Marseille via Court de Gébelin.
sections.In this library: ✅ Rewritten in this library + live in Supabase.
sections.Scene:
Etteilla I — the Livre de Thot — is the founding document of divinatory tarot, designed by the French cartomancer Etteilla (Jean-Baptiste Alliette) and published in successive editions from 1789 onward.

Etteilla fundamentally redesigned the tarot. He renumbered the cards, placing card 1 (The Questioner — Le Chaos/Etteilla) at the head of the deck. He replaced many standard Marseille images with new ones keyed to his cosmological system, which read the 78 cards as an encoded account of the Genesis creation narrative. He added keywords and reversed meanings to each card.

The Livre de Thot was the first deck designed specifically for fortune-telling rather than for game-playing — the first tarot that was a divination instrument from conception rather than a game that was secondarily adapted to divination. All subsequent tarot divination, including the Rider-Waite-Smith system, descends from the questions Etteilla raised about how to build a coherent symbolic system for reading cards.

sections.What it is: Later editions of Etteilla's system — Blocquel's 'Julia Orsini' (II) and Regamey's chromolithograph 'Oracle des Dames' (III).
sections.Lineage: Continuations of Etteilla's deck.
sections.Scene:
Etteilla II and III are the subsequent editions and adaptations of Etteilla's Livre de Thot produced by his followers in the late 18th and early 19th centuries.

The Etteilla II deck (the "Egyptian" or "Grand Etteilla") extended his cosmological system and refined the imagery. The Etteilla III (Oracle des Dames) adapted the system for a broader popular market, particularly emphasising romantic and domestic divination scenarios — a feminisation of the oracle that reflects the growing role of women as the primary consumers of fortune-telling services in 19th-century France.

Together the three Etteilla editions show how a single innovative system proliferates and mutates through successive hands. The core ideas — numbered card positions, reversed meanings, explicit keyword systems — remain, but the imagery and emphasis drift as different editors and publishers make the deck their own.

sections.What it is: Lévi wrote no deck but mapped the 22 trumps onto the 22 Hebrew letters and the Kabbalistic Tree of Life — the framework all later esoteric tarot uses.
sections.Lineage: A tradition NODE: the Kabbalistic key, inherited by Wirth and the Golden Dawn.
sections.Scene:
Éliphas Lévi (1810–1875) never produced a complete tarot deck, but his theoretical writings on tarot — particularly in Dogme et Rituel de la Haute Magie (1854–1856) — were the most influential force in establishing tarot as a serious esoteric system.

Lévi's key move was connecting the 22 Major Arcana to the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet and to the 22 paths of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life. This connection — which Lévi seems to have invented, or at least formulated for the first time in writing — became the foundation of all subsequent Western occult tarot. The Golden Dawn built its entire tarot system on Lévi's framework. Waite acknowledged Lévi as a primary source. Crowley, who believed himself to be a reincarnation of Lévi, pushed the Kabbalistic framework still further.

The tarot images attributed to Lévi are mostly his disciples' reconstructions of what he described in his books, not a complete deck he designed himself.

sections.What it is: Gérard Encausse ('Papus') systematised the Lévi correspondences in a famous book, illustrated with Wirth's majors.
sections.Lineage: A tradition NODE linking Lévi to Wirth.
sections.Scene:
Gérard Encausse, writing under the pen name Papus (1865–1916), was the most systematic French occultist of the late 19th century — the author of the Traité Méthodique de Science Occulte (1891) and the Tarot des Bohémiens (1889), which remained the standard French-language introduction to esoteric tarot for several decades.

Papus inherited Lévi's Kabbalistic framework and extended it into a comprehensive system that mapped the 78 cards onto the Hebrew alphabet, the sephiroth of the Tree of Life, the four suits to the four letters of IHVH (Yod-He-Vau-He), and each card to a specific divinatory meaning for upright and reversed positions.

The Tarot des Bohémiens was accompanied by illustrations produced by Gabriel Goulinat but the deck was never commercially released as a standalone product in Papus's lifetime. His influence is felt primarily through the book rather than through a specific deck design.

sections.What it is: Wirth designed 22 esoteric Major Arcana under Stanislas de Guaita — reworked Marseille imagery carrying Lévi's Hebrew-letter and astrological symbols.
sections.Lineage: Bridges Lévi's Kabbalah into imagery; the Continental esoteric major arcana.
sections.Scene: A representative image from Oswald Wirth's 1889 tarot — designed with guidance from Stanislas de Guaita and published as 22 major arcana cards — shows the first tarot deck explicitly designed for esoteric practice. Wirth's cards are redesigned Marseille trumps with Kabbalistic correspondences added: Hebrew letters, astrological symbols, and Masonic imagery incorporated into the illustrations. The aesthetic is art nouveau before art nouveau's full flowering — refined engraving in the French symbolist style, the figures more dynamic and symbolically charged than the Marseille originals. The High Priestess or the Magician might appear with explicit Hebrew letter attributions, the first visual encoding of the Hermetic Qabalah in tarot form.
sections.Symbol: Wirth's 1889 occult Majors — Hebrew letters and esoteric symbols added to the Marseille template, the first tarot explicitly designed for Kabbalistic practice.

sections.What it is: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn's complete correspondence system (Book T): paths, decans, Sephiroth, 'Lord of…' titles. It swaps Strength↔Justice.
sections.Lineage: The English synthesis; parent of the Rider-Waite-Smith and Thoth decks.
sections.Scene: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn's tarot system (London, 1888-1900) was documented in members' initiation materials rather than published as a commercial deck. A representative image in this context is likely a later reconstruction or the Book T diagrams — diagrammatic descriptions of tarot card designs with elaborate Hermetic attributions, or a painted card from a Golden Dawn initiate's private deck. The Golden Dawn system assigned each card to the Tree of Life and an astrological or elemental attribution, redesigned the court card hierarchy (King, Queen, Prince, Princess), and systematized the entire 78-card correspondence structure that underlies both the Thoth and Rider-Waite-Smith decks.
sections.Symbol: The Golden Dawn's Book T system — the Hermetic attribution framework that underlies both the Thoth and RWS decks, documented for initiates rather than published commercially.

sections.What it is: A.E. Waite & Pamela Colman Smith's deck — the first to fully illustrate the minor arcana with scenes. The most influential modern tarot.
sections.Lineage: Descends from the Golden Dawn; ancestor of thousands of modern decks.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the Rider-Waite-Smith Tarot (1909, published by Rider Company, London) — probably The Fool (0) or The Star (XVII) or The World (XXI) — shows Pamela Colman Smith's revolutionary scenic illustration style: every card, including all 40 pip cards, shows a fully illustrated scene rather than geometric pip arrangements. The watercolor-based artwork in a simple, accessible style draws on pre-Raphaelite aesthetics, medieval imagery, and Pamela Colman Smith's Caribbean and theatrical background. The Fool stepping off the cliff, the Hermit holding his lantern, the Page of Cups with his fish — scenes that have become the visual vocabulary of tarot for 100 million people.
sections.Symbol: Pamela Colman Smith's 1909 scenic pip cards — the revolutionary decision to illustrate all 78 cards with narrative scenes, making the Waite-Smith the most influential tarot deck of all time.

sections.What it is: Aleister Crowley and Lady Frieda Harris's esoteric deck — the culmination of the Golden Dawn line.
sections.Lineage: Descends from the Golden Dawn. ⛔ Still in copyright — referenced here, never reproduced.
sections.Scene:
The Thoth Tarot, painted by Lady Frieda Harris under the direction of Aleister Crowley between 1938 and 1943, is the most technically complex tarot ever produced — a 78-card synthesis of Thelemic theology, astrology, Kabbalah, Egyptian mythology, and projective geometry.

Crowley renamed and reconceptualised several cards: Justice became Adjustment, Strength became Lust, the World became the Universe, and the Fool's position in the system was theoretically elevated. Harris's paintings use projective geometry (she had studied under Rudolf Steiner's followers) to create three-dimensional spatial effects on the flat card surface.

The Thoth deck was not commercially published until 1969, after both creators had died. It has since become the most influential tarot of the second half of the 20th century among serious occult practitioners, and its imagery has generated an enormous body of commentary. Its chief rival for cultural influence is the Rider-Waite-Smith deck, which preceded it by 34 years and reached a much wider popular audience.

sections.What it is: The earliest COMPLETE 78-card illustrated deck — trumps named for classical figures (Mato, Panfilio, Catulo…), steeped in alchemical and humanist symbolism. Pamela Colman Smith studied its pips.
sections.Lineage: A sui-generis offshoot, in none of the three orders; an alchemical art-object more than a game deck.
sections.Scene:
The Sola Busca Tarot (Venice, 1491) is the oldest fully illustrated tarot deck in existence — the only 15th-century deck in which all 78 cards, including the pip cards, bear individual figured scenes.

Engraved in intaglio (not woodcut), the Sola Busca draws on Roman history and classical mythology for its imagery — each pip card shows a different historical or mythological scene, and the court cards and trumps continue this Roman thread. The trumps are named for historical figures (Nerone, Caligola, Marte, Mercurio) rather than the allegorical figures of the Marseille tradition.

The deck was almost certainly known to Pamela Colman Smith when she designed the Rider-Waite-Smith pip cards in 1909 — the British Museum had acquired photographic reproductions of the Sola Busca in 1907, and several RWS pip scenes appear to directly quote Sola Busca compositions. This makes the Sola Busca a hidden structural ancestor of the most widely used tarot in the world.

sections.What it is: A 50-card series of educational engravings (muses, virtues, planets, estates) — NOT a tarot at all, despite the name; it has no suits and no trick-taking trumps.
sections.Lineage: A famous false relative, included to clear up the confusion.
sections.In this library: ✅ Built in this library (grammars/mantegna-tarocchi) — grammar_type 'custom', not 'tarot'.
sections.Scene: A representative image from the 'Mantegna Tarocchi' (Ferrara, c. 1465, misattributed to Mantegna but actually anonymous) shows one of the 50 engraved copper-plate prints that constitute this famous educational series. Not a game tarot but a humanist instructional tool, the Mantegna Tarocchi depicts five groups of ten figures: the social estates (Beggar through Pope), the nine Muses and Apollo, the Liberal Arts, the cosmic principles (Genius through Chronos), and the celestial spheres. Each figure is depicted as a standing allegorical form in classical Italian Renaissance engraving style — crisp outlines, elegant drapery, Latin labels — on a small card-sized format that may have inspired later tarot imagery.
sections.Symbol: The 50-card Mantegna Tarocchi engravings — not a game tarot but a humanist educational series, its classical allegorical hierarchy a parallel universe to the tarot tradition.

sections.What it is: The thousands of modern decks descending from the Marseille, RWS, or Thoth — including the library's own community decks (Tarocchino Arlecchino, Clown Town, Anecdotes, Luiza Lian, the Ontoject…).
sections.Lineage: The living edge of the tree; nearly all trace to RWS or the Marseille.
sections.Scene:
The category of modern derivative decks encompasses the extraordinary proliferation of tarot since the mid-20th century — from the initial RWS-influenced wave of the 1970s and 1980s through the explosion of themed, illustrated, and artist-designed decks in the digital age.

By the early 21st century, thousands of tarot decks are in commercial circulation, ranging from faithful reproductions of historical decks to completely original artistic visions that retain only the 78-card structure and some memory of the traditional trump sequence.

The most significant design traditions that have emerged in this period include: the Thoth school (abstract, Kabbalistic, astrological); the neo-Marseille school (returning to traditional imagery with updated production values); the RWS-clone school (variations on the Waite-Smith imagery for specific communities or aesthetics); and the artist's deck school (where the tarot structure is a framework for individual creative expression). The modern derivative category is where tarot's living future is being made.

sections.About this branch: The earliest surviving tarots: luxurious hand-painted decks made for the Visconti and Sforza courts of Milan. Tarot begins here as a card GAME (trionfi), not divination. These Milanese decks seed what Dummett calls the C-order.
sections.Decks here: Visconti-Sforza Tarot, Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone), Brera-Brambilla
sections.Scene:
The Roots branch gathers the founding decks of tarot — the hand-painted luxury objects commissioned by the ruling dynasties of Milan and Ferrara in the 1440s.

These are not playing cards in any ordinary sense. They are objects of court prestige, painted by skilled artists on heavy vellum and sometimes gilded. Compared to later Marseille cards they are larger, more variable in iconography, and deeply tied to the specific symbolism of their patron families.

The Visconti-Sforza family of Milan produced the largest surviving group. The Este court at Ferrara produced the d'Este tarot. The Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) may be the oldest surviving deck of any kind. All share the same basic structure — four suits of fourteen cards, plus a sequence of allegorical trump figures — but no two early decks are identical in the ordering or imagery of the trumps. Standardisation comes later, through printing.

sections.About this branch: One of the three regional trump orders (Dummett's A). The southern tradition, home of the Bolognese Tarocchino and the vast Florentine Minchiate. Note the four interchangeable 'Papi' replacing the Popess/Empress/Emperor/Pope.
sections.Decks here: Tarocchino di Bologna, Minchiate (Florence), Tarocco Siciliano
sections.Scene:
The Southern Way traces the distinctive tarot traditions of Bologna and Florence — cities that preserved older trump orderings and developed their own variant forms long after the Marseille type had standardised the north.

Bologna produced the Tarocchino, a reduced 62-card game in which the lowest pip cards (2–5) were removed and four of the standard trumps were replaced by four interchangeable "Moor" figures called Papi. The Bolognese order places the virtues in different positions from the Marseille standard.

Florence produced the most radical variant of all: the Minchiate, a 97-card expansion of the standard deck that added the twelve signs of the zodiac, the four elements, and the three theological virtues to the 22 standard trumps. It was played as a sophisticated trick-taking game and remained popular in Florence into the 19th century. Both traditions show that for two centuries "tarot" was not a single fixed system but a family of related games.

sections.About this branch: The eastern trump order (Dummett's B), associated with the d'Este court of Ferrara. Known mostly from fragments and the so-called 'Charles VI' deck.
sections.Scene:
The Eastern Way marks the Ferrara branch of tarot — the B-order decks, named for the ordering of their trumps identified by scholars as the "Ferrara type."

In the B-order, cards are numbered differently from the later Marseille standard. The most significant differences involve Justice and Strength: in the B-order Justice appears at position VIII and Strength (Fortitude) at XI — the opposite of the A-order (Marseille) convention in which Strength is VIII and Justice XI. The Golden Dawn and Rider-Waite-Smith decks later revived the B-order positions, giving them new occult rationale.

The Ferrara court produced the d'Este tarot and was also the setting of Boiardo's tarocchi poem (c. 1494), which describes a completely different allegorical tarot game made for the Este family — evidence that in Ferrara tarot was understood as a literary and philosophical game as much as a card game.

items.name: The Western Way — Milan to Marseille (C-order)

sections.About this branch: The western trump order (Dummett's C) that, through Milan and France, became the printed STANDARD — the Tarot de Marseille — and its regional cousins. This is the trunk from which the occult decks branch.
sections.Decks here: Tarot de Marseille — Conver 1760, Marseille Type I — Noblet / Dodal, Tarot de Besançon / Swiss 1JJ, Belgian / Vandenborre Tarot, Tarocco Piemontese
sections.Scene:
The Western Way is the great trunk of the tarot tradition — the Marseille type, which from the 16th to the 19th century was the dominant form of tarot across France, Switzerland, northern Italy, and beyond.

The Marseille deck standardised the imagery that all later occultists, artists, and psychologists would work from: the Bateleur at his table, the Papesse with her book, the Chariot's armoured conqueror, the Hanged Man suspended by one foot, the Moon's crayfish crawling toward the towers. These images were not designed — they evolved through the collective hands of dozens of woodcutters and printers over two centuries, each copying and modifying the generation before them.

The key decks of this branch include the Noblet (Paris, c. 1650), the Viéville (Paris, c. 1650 — with an older Eastern trump order), the Dodal (Lyon, c. 1701), the Conver (Marseille, 1760 — the standard reference), the Tarot de Besançon (Swiss variant that substituted Juno and Jupiter for the Papesse and Pope), and the Belgian / Vandenborre deck. All share the same essential imagery; the differences are in fine detail, colour, and a handful of iconographic choices.

sections.About this branch: From the 1780s the Tarot de Marseille was reinterpreted as ancient esoteric wisdom — Court de Gébelin's Egyptian myth, Etteilla's cartomancy, Lévi's Kabbalah, and then the English decks (Golden Dawn, Rider-Waite-Smith, Thoth). Divination is INVENTED on this branch.
sections.Decks here: Court de Gébelin's Plates, Etteilla I — Livre de Thot, Etteilla II & III (editions), Éliphas Lévi, Papus — Tarot des Bohémiens, Oswald Wirth Tarot, Golden Dawn — Book T, Rider-Waite-Smith, Thoth (Crowley–Harris)
sections.Scene:
The Occult Reframing is the transformation of tarot from a card game into a system of esoteric wisdom — one of the most consequential reinventions in the history of symbolism.

It begins with Antoine Court de Gébelin's famous 1781 claim in Monde Primitif that the tarot was an ancient Egyptian book of wisdom brought to Europe by the Gypsies. The claim was entirely invented, but it unlocked something: for the first time, people read the tarot images as intentional symbols pointing at hidden truths rather than as conventional game imagery.

Etteilla (1783) was the first to design a tarot specifically for divination, renumbering and redesigning the cards around his system. Éliphas Lévi (1854) connected tarot to the Kabbalah and the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet. The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn systematised these correspondences into a complete esoteric system. Arthur Edward Waite and Pamela Colman Smith (1909) produced the Rider-Waite-Smith deck — the first to illustrate all 78 cards with full narrative scenes — which became the template for almost every 20th-century tarot. Crowley and Harris's Thoth deck (1943) pushed the occult symbolism still further, into astrology, the Tree of Life, and Thelemic theology.

sections.About this branch: Decks that stand apart from the three orders, plus the relatives constantly confused with tarot: the alchemical Sola Busca, the Mantegna 'Tarocchi' (not actually a tarot), and the modern derivative decks.
sections.Decks here: Sola Busca, Mantegna 'Tarocchi', Modern Derivative Decks
sections.Scene:
The Sui Generis branch collects decks that resist easy classification — decks from outside the main European tarot tradition, or decks that represent isolated experiments without direct descendants.

The Sola Busca Tarot (Venice, 1491) is the oldest surviving fully illustrated 78-card deck — every single card, including the pip cards, bears a unique figured scene. It was engraved in metal, not woodcut, and its imagery draws on classical mythology and Roman history rather than the allegories of the Marseille tradition. Pamela Colman Smith almost certainly used it as a source when designing the RWS minor arcana scenes.

The Mantegna Tarocchi (c. 1465) are not a tarot deck at all in the game sense — they are a set of 50 educational prints illustrating the cosmic order, from the lowest social ranks to the celestial spheres, made in the workshop tradition of Andrea Mantegna. They influenced the symbolic vocabulary of later tarot without being tarot themselves.

sections.The Whole Tree: Tarot began as a card GAME in mid-1400s Northern Italy — a fifth suit of 22 illustrated 'triumphs' added to an ordinary pack. From that single root three regional trump-orders diverged (Dummett's A, B, C), the western C-order hardened into the printed Tarot de Marseille, and from the 1780s the Marseille was reimagined as esoteric wisdom, producing the occult decks. Divination is a late branch, not the root.
sections.How to read this tree: Each branch is a lineage; each leaf is a deck. Crucially, the Marseille (C-order) and the Bolognese Tarocchino (A-order) are COUSINS — both children of the common Italian root — not parent and child. Follow a branch down to see what descends from what.
sections.Scene:
The Tree of Tarot maps six centuries of tarot history as a living genealogy — not a fixed timeline but an evolving tradition that branches, hybridises, and reinvents itself while remaining recognisably itself.

Tarot begins in the courts of northern Italy in the 1440s as a luxury card game for aristocrats. By the late 16th century it has spread across Europe and diverged into several regional families. In the late 18th century Parisian occultists seize on it as a system of secret wisdom, and in the early 20th century English initiates redesign it from the ground up. Today hundreds of variants are in circulation.

The tree has five main branches: the Roots (early Italian luxury decks), the Southern Way (Bologna and Florence), the Western Way (the great Marseille tradition), the Occult Reframing (Etteilla, Levi, Waite, Crowley), and the Sui Generis branch (decks that don't fit any lineage cleanly). Each branch is a distinct answer to the question: what is a tarot, and what is it for?

sections.What it is: The original thing: a trick-taking card GAME invented in 1440s northern Italy, when a fifth suit of allegorical ‘triumphs’ (trionfi) was added to an ordinary four-suit pack so the trumps could beat the suits. Every later tarot — game or divinatory — descends from this.
sections.Lineage: The common root. The A (Florence/Bologna), B (Ferrara) and C (Milan→Marseille) trump-orders all branch from here; the occult reframing splits off the C line only after 1781.
sections.In this library: The conceptual root node — not a single surviving deck but the game all the dated decks below descend from.

sections.What it is: A contemporary, playful, philosophical deck (Playful Process). It borrows the 78-card shape but makes no esoteric or divinatory claim — so on this tree it descends from the GAME, not the occult line.
sections.Lineage: Playful / non-esoteric. Placed off the Italian trionfi root rather than off Rider-Waite-Smith, because it does not inherit (or claim) the occult correspondence system.

sections.What it is: A contemporary deck that lays a poetic, narrative reading over the familiar 78-card scenes — an interpretive overlay in the same spirit as the Rider-Waite-Smith pictures, so here it descends from RWS.
sections.Lineage: Poetic / interpretive. Branches off Rider-Waite-Smith because it leans on RWS’s scenic, meaning-bearing image vocabulary.

_pending_decks.name: Tarocchino Arlecchino
_pending_decks.note: Descends from the Tarocchino di Bologna it is named for. Community deck; held until finished.

_pending_decks.name: Clown Town
_pending_decks.note: Playful, no esoteric claim → off the game root. Community deck; held until finished.

_pending_decks.name: Arlecchino's Augmented Arcana
_pending_decks.note: Playful, no esoteric claim → off the game root. Community deck; held until finished.
